title: Star Wars: Episode VI - Return of the Jedi

segments.title: 幕后揭秘 / Behind the Scenes
segments.summary: 《绝地归来》制作团队分享电影创作历程，讲述导演选择、视觉与音效设计挑战，重点揭秘贾巴角色如何从概念到银幕的实现过程，展现电影艺术与技术融合的幕后故事。

segments.title: 幕后匠心 / Behind the Scenes
segments.summary: 本段落展现了《绝地归来》的幕后创作过程，包括特效制作、声音设计和技术决策。导演卢卡斯、音效师伯特和特效总监蒂佩特分享了汉·索洛角色的转变、贾巴宫殿场景的设计挑战以及如何通过创新技术使异形角色栩栩如生，体现了电影制作者们对细节的极致追求。

segments.title: 真相与觉醒 / Truth and Awakening
segments.summary: 卢克在尤达指导下面对维达是其父亲的真实性，完成绝地训练的关键时刻。尽管他仍未完全掌握绝地之道，但已准备好面对与父亲的终极对决，同时电影展现了制作团队如何通过复杂场景和特效呈现这一史诗性结局。

segments.title: 幕后揭秘 / Behind the Secrets
segments.summary: 这段字幕揭示了《绝地归来》制作过程中的技术细节与叙事策略，重点解释了卢克与莱娅作为双胞胎的重要情节设定，以及通过特效技术创造史诗感的方法。制作团队分享了音效设计、混音创新与特效突破的经验，展示了电影工业不断拓展技术边界的追求。

segments.title: 幕后匠心 / Behind the Magic
segments.summary: 这段字幕展现了《星球大战：绝地归来》中飞车追逐场景和伊沃克人的幕后创作过程。电影人讲述如何利用实景拍摄和模型创造动感森林追逐，声音设计师从日常生活采集声音增强真实感。卢卡斯解释了他如何将伍基人转变为伊沃克人，以及演员如何通过表演赋予这些外星生物真实性格。

segments.title: 伊沃克创造术 / Ewok Creation
segments.summary: 本段落展现了伊沃克人的创作历程，包括从一位中国老人的声音采集到完整语言系统的构建，以及演员们如何通过特殊技巧赋予这些毛茸茸生物生动的表达。同时探讨电影创作者在特效受限条件下如何解决角色表现力的挑战。

segments.title: 父子对峙 / Father and Son Confrontation
segments.summary: 卢克选择不逃避与父亲达斯·维达的情感对峙，通过直接挑战表达决心。电影中呈现了小团体对抗强权帝国的主题，Ewok的战斗需要平衡可信度与幽默感。制作团队通过精心设计和特效展现了这场终极对决。

segments.title: 黑暗诱惑 / Dark Temptation
segments.summary: 卢克在死星上面对皇帝致命的诱惑，皇帝试图激怒他杀死维达，使其转向黑暗面。同时，伊沃克人与帝国军队激战，协助摧毁护盾发生器。这是电影中关于抵抗愤怒、坚守光明面的关键对决。

segments.title: 维达救赎 / Vader's Redemption
segments.summary: 本段落探讨了《绝地归来》高潮场景的制作细节，包括维达与皇帝的最终对决、维达救赎的关键时刻以及电影结局的创意过程。卢克焚烧父亲遗体原本不在剧本中，但为故事提供了更完整的情感闭合，展现了维达从黑暗到光明的转变历程。

subtitles.text: This is George Lucas. I'm the executive producer
and writer of Return of the Jedi.
I'm Dennis Muren, visual effects supervisor at ILM.
Hi. I'm Carrie Fisher.
And this is Ben Burtt, sound designer and supervising sound editor...
and also rerecording mixer on...
Return of the Jedi.
LUCAS: Richard Marquand was the director of the film.
He'd done some great suspense films
and was really good at working with actors.
Eye of the Needle was the film I'd seen that he had done
that impressed me most.
It was really nicely done, had a lot of energy and suspense.
These movies, by this time, had gotten to be more like television shows.
The hierarchy in the way a television show works, creatively,
is very much different than the way it works on a motion picture.
The executive producer pretty much
is the overall creative overseer of the project...
and comes up with the ideas for the scripts and works on the scripts.
And the directors just try to fulfill the obligation
of getting it on the screen.
Where, mostly in feature films, a lot of the creative control lies in the director.
It's not so much today as it was then,
but I've discovered over the years that it's easy to collaborate with directors...
if there's mutual respect about what you're doing...
and a kind of general agreement on the sensibility
of where you're going with something...
or what kind of a film it's gonna be or what the tone of it is gonna be.
If you can agree on those sorts of things in the beginning before you start off,
it works out pretty well.
I had a great time working with Richard Marquand.
He was a very talented director and a very nice guy.
He'd never done a film of this scale before,
with special effects and all that sort of thing...
so, just as on Empire Strikes Back...
I was there to assist and help...
and sort of give my expertise.
These films are so huge...
that it's really hard to do it by yourself.
I mean, you really need to have a lot of people to support you in all the areas.
Fortunately in this one,
especially in the art department and a lot of the other departments,
we were using the same group that we'd used before.
In special effects, it was the same group.
So it was easier for me to shorthand with a lot of people,
and that became a real asset for Richard...
because I could say, "Don't worry about that. We'll take care of it."
A lot of the technical complications
and everything he didn't even have to worry about.
He just focused on, you know, getting the performances out of the actors...
and making sure the story got told on the set.
We had that cooperative relationship...
where if I said, "Just do it like this and don't worry about it,"
he was curious about what was gonna happen,
but he didn't spend a lot of time...
trying to reinvent the wheel or say, "No, I don't wanna do it that way."
Because he'd say, "Look, if this is the way you do this, it's much easier.
Let's just do this 'cause that'll all be a matte painting anyway."
It's very easy, especially at this stage in special effects
where it was relatively new,
to get hung up trying to figure out how things were gonna actually come out.
The only way you ever really know is having gone through it a few times.
Then you can sort of anticipate where everything's gonna be.
A film like this operates on a lot of faith. The actors have to have faith,
and in this case the director had to have a lot of faith
that it would actually come together...
and all the missing pieces would mysteriously appear in post-production.
BURTT: The footsteps for Darth Vader
as he comes down the ramp...
were recorded in underground tunnels...
in the Marin Headlands near the Golden Gate Bridge.
There's a network of underground military tunnels...
which are actually closed off from the public.
But back at the time that Jedi was done...
you could squeeze through one of the old, rusty gates...
and go deep down into the catacombs...
into the headlands around the Golden Gate Bridge.
Gary Summers and I took our tape recorder and some heavy boots...
and some other props and we went down into these tunnels
and did a lot of the footsteps-
echoey, heavy footsteps for Darth Vader.
Along the way, we also got some great
rusty doors opening and closing,
which were used for various sounds of ramps and gates...
and things that happen in Jedi.
There's a lot of machinery in every Star Wars film,
and we could never collect enough squeaky doors and electric motors...
in order to make up all the different sounds that occur in the spaceships...
and all the places that we go.
LUCAS: It's the final chapter...
of the original concept for Star Wars.
There were a lot of things that were...
added into this one because of the fact that...
by the time I got down this far from the original script...
the story had become pretty thin and I had to fill in a lot of blanks.
This first whole sequence with Jabba the Hutt...
was more an afterthought than anything else.
The original story was really about the primitive society...
overcoming the Empire at the end and...
Luke managing to redeem his father.
The part of the script this actually represented in the very first outline,
where I had this whole story outlined,
was like the last 20 pages of the script.
So I had to come up with another 100 pages of stuff...
to fill in the blanks and make it work...
because Han Solo had become such a popular character.
And I thought it'd be fun to go back to Tatooine.
And I wanted to deal with Jabba the Hutt,
which kinda got lost in the process.
He was a much bigger character in the original script.
A lot of those things motivated me to go in this direction...
and spend the first quarter of the movie back on Tatooine with Jabba the Hutt.
BURTT: In Jedi, we had quite a bit
of Huttese language to create.
This was the language spoken by Jabba the Hutt
and all the people in his palace.
It starts out with this little eye robot that pops out the door.
We used the talents of Larry Ward, who was a linguist.
He was an expert at speaking many different languages,
and he helped me develop the Huttese language back on New Hope.
In fact, Larry had done the voice for Greedo the bounty hunter.
But Larry's biggest contribution to this film...
was by doing the voice of Jabba the Hutt.
LUCAS: As with all these movies,
the original intent...
was to make this a very full and exotic environment...
that these movies would take place in.
And so, each film we tried to push the envelope a little bit further...
in terms of unique characters...
and take it into a more visually exciting...
and unusual area.
The problem always was, to do that is very difficult.
And especially before digital technology,
if you had to have exotic robots and things,
they were mostly marionettes...
or stop-motion or something of that ilk.
So we were just pushing the limits of cinematic technology at that point...
to just get strange creatures.
The first two films were pretty light on creatures.
We had a little bit in the cantina in Episode IV,
and in Episode V we put most of our effort and focus on Yoda...
trying to get Yoda to become real.
Once we managed to make Yoda work and become real,
then we, on this film, tried to expand that idea...
and make Jabba and the rancor...
and the people in Jabba's court- the pig guards, the strange droids-
to make it a much more exotic environment
and use them more.
We barely, barely, barely were able to
put together the very first cantina scene.
We shot it, then had to go reshoot it and make more creatures, and reshoot it...
and constantly trying to bring it up to the level that
I had originally hoped it would be.
And so each one of these kind of exotic scenes-
there's a huge amount of work behind every single idea...
to try to get to the level where you can create an alien environment...
on the level of just not having to think about it.
It's one thing to do a creature.
It's another thing to do 35 or 40 or 50 creatures in each scene.
MUREN: This is Jabba the Hutt, who was designed, I believe,
by Phil Tippett here at ILM when he was here.
We had quite a large creature shop, actually, at ILM at the time...
building a lot of characters for Jedi here.
Then they got shipped over to England where they were shot.
It was a great big rubber creature with a number of people behind it,
inside of it, and moving around.
LUCAS: There's a dwarf in the tail...
and then a couple guys on remote controls
running the tongue and the eyes.
They have to rehearse and rehearse and rehearse
to get all the movements right...
so that he'll do the right thing 'cause they're all
trying to coordinate their moves.
The group came mostly out of Jim Henson's group,
which is probably the best in terms of just sheer talent and skills...
of working on puppets and puppeting...
especially as everything has advanced
to more remote-controlled puppeteering.
They were the ones that led most of that technology.
MUREN: Some of these little characters-
This little character here is a little rod puppet, I believe,
and some mechanics inside of it to make it look spindly and kind of mobile.
With computer graphics, you can do a lot more...
but in those days, we were pretty limited
to the motions that we could do with these characters.
BURTT: To give Jabba the deepest and heaviest sound,
we took Larry's voice and we pitched it almost an octave lower
than his natural voice.
And then we ran the voice through a subharmonic generator...
and it gave his voice a very booming sound,
a very heavy sound.
And then, while he spoke, a movement track was created,
which was really just cheese casserole.
Running your hands around in a bowl of cheese casserole...
gave it a smooshy, kind of moist feeling for Jabba's movements...
and a slurpiness to his voice.
But also we took a wet towel...
that had sort of been thrown in the mud-
You take that wet towel and work with it inside the bottom of a garbage can...
such that the sound is kinda contained and has a little bit of a quick echo to it.
Working your hands on the towel to give gritty, sliding sounds,
and also combining with the cheese casserole,
were used to underscore all of Jabba's talking and movements.
I always found that with these- really, essentially a large puppet,
that you needed to get some kind of grit,
some kind of subtle noise into it when he spoke...
in order to make it seem more present.
Those of us on the crew almost always take turns...
doing incidental voices in the film.
The interrogator robot- the voice was performed
by the director, Richard Marquand.
We ran his voice through a circuit called a ring modulator,
which gave it kind of a monotonic, electronic quality to it.
I got tortured in this film doing the voice for the robot
having his feet burned off.
This brings up a serious issue of, do robots feel pain? Well, I guess so.
No! No! No!
Aaahh!
The whole scene of Oola dancing...
has gone through several different evolutionary changes.
There was originally some music done for the first release of the film...
music which we actually recorded at Sprocket Systems.
The lyrics were written by one of our engineers named Annie Arbogast,
who had her own punk rock band.
I worked with her, advising her on how to make it sound like Huttese.
But she did the original voice and wrote the lyrics.
But then years later, when the film was re-released in a special edition,
a whole new piece of music was written
when they added all the digital characters...
and, you know, really elaborated the whole scene.
LUCAS: A lot of these things went through a lot of incarnations.
The singer, originally, was a marionette we could barely move...
and really couldn't do what I had wanted.
Some of the other characters who were more static
were able to come off pretty well,
but Jabba can't really move in this one-he's stuck on a slab.
The piano player and some of those people are stuck on slabs
because they're puppets.
So it's as you push the technology forward
you're able to get the creatures...
suddenly to walk or raise their arms or have expressions on their faces.
And all that took years and years and a lot of experimentation...
and technical advances to just be able to get an alien character to be real.
In this particular case, you know, make the tongue work.
[LAUGHS] Or the eyes work- That was like a major breakthrough...
to be able to make eyelids move up and down
and eyes move back and forth...
and the tongue go in and out and the lips be relatively in sync.
I'm still amused by people that somehow think...
that when you use cyber-technology, or digital technology, in movies...
that suddenly it's... fake.
But when you look at a scene in here, like Jabba's palace,
now there's some digital characters in here,
but they're no more or less fake
than all the other characters that are in here.
I mean, is a digital character more fake than a big, fat rubber character?
[CHUCKLES] I mean, there's nothing real here at all.
It's hard to say that a rubber character has more integrity
than a digital character.
What I try to do is just make the characters become believable...
so they look realistic enough to where you have a suspension of disbelief...
and accept them as characters, not as tricks, which is what they all are.
BURTT: The voice of Boushh-
Of course, it's Princess Leia in disguise,
but when she first appears we used a different voice for the character.
The character was actually voiced by Pat Welsh.
She was the woman who was the principal voice...
of ET the Extra-Terrestrial.
I'd worked on ET, and we had recorded her voice.
She was an elderly woman, retired,
never had acted in any films before.
I had run into her at a photo store in San Anselmo, California,
while she was buying film.
I heard her voice,
and her voice was very interesting and unusual...
in that, to some extent, you couldn't tell whether it was male or female.
One of the keys in doing alien voices...
is always to come up with a voice...
in which the audience cannot immediately tell
whether it's male or female...
or old or young, so they don't link it up with a familiar human being.
So, when you find voices like that...
that have that ambiguous quality about them,
they're so useful in these fantasy films.
Well, Pat had a voice like that, and I just asked her,
"Do you want to be the voice of an alien?"
And she looked at me and she didn't even-didn't flinch
or call the police or anything.
She said- [IMITATING PAT] "Okay. I'll try it."
So we recorded her and worked with her over the years.
We became good friends and used her on occasion for specialty voices.
LUCAS: Lando moved through into this film...
primarily by my decision to focus
the beginning of this movie on Han Solo.
Since they were pals, it was natural that Lando would be one...
to try to redeem himself and be there to help rescue him...
and redeem his reputation at the same time.
Ultimately, in the original original script...
it was mostly Luke, the emperor and Darth Vader...
were the key elements along with what, in the original script,
was a bunch of Wookiees.
It was a Wookiee planet.
But since I had fallen in love with the Wookiee so much...
when I made Episode IV I decided to make the co-pilot a Wookiee,
which meant he was technologically advanced.
The whole concept originally was that the people that overthrew
the Empire were not technological.
So I had to reinvent a half-sized Wookiee.
BURTT: We always get a lot of mileage out of Dumpsters in these films-
banging into Dumpsters, slamming the lids on Dumpsters.
The carbon block in which Han is encased...
when it falls on the floor, it's the sound of a big garbage Dumpster...
recorded out in front of my home
to give it the sense of weight and a metallic quality.
MUREN: The shot of Han breaking out of this carbon thing,
whatever it is he's in, was pretty tough.
It was, uh-What you're seeing is actually a wax figure of Han,
of the real casting that was made, but of wax, animated,
cut away one frame at a time with a bright light on the inside, shot in smoke.
So it was a pretty bizarre thing to figure out just how to get him to look
like this thing was peeling off of him.
And we did it with, again-with sort of revealing bright light on the inside,
which makes absolutely no sense.
And it's been copied in many, many films afterwards...
when they've had somebody sort of covered with something...
and it's showing it having to peel off.
It's an interesting way to do it, sort of as though it's glowing on the inside.
But there's no logic to it.
LUCAS: The Han Solo journey...
is a continuation of the motif of death and rebirth,
which is the overall central theme of this whole series...
in relationship to Darth Vader...
coming out of his evil hibernation...
and being reborn as Anakin Skywalker.
He's put to sleep, which is a common device,
especially in fairy tales, but also in mythological terms too,
of going into a state of suspended animation.
And then usually you come out rejuvenated.
Sometimes it's the equivalent of going down to the netherworld...
and coming back enlightened.
In this particular case, he was just put to sleep.
Ho-ho-ho-ho-ho!
What's that?
BURTT: When Jabba and his minions laugh,
it's a real combination of human laughter
plus other animal sounds like hippos...
and hyenas and other things we've recorded or collected over the years...
all blended together to give a whole range of alien laughter.
LUCAS: The difficulty of these scenes in Jabba's palace,
especially with Jabba, is that Jabba can't move.
I didn't have the technology at that time to make him move around,
so whenever I had to do a scene I had to plop him down somewhere...
and then stage the scene around him,
which made for this inconvenient staging
in the revelation scene here...
of him having to be behind a curtain.
Otherwise, he could just come through the door,
which is what I would have preferred.
But these were the days when most of the creatures were nailed to the floor.
BURTT: Sometimes you can create the ambience
for a place in a very simple way.
A lot of the sound in the detention cell is dripping water,
but really it was just taking an eyedropper...
and doing single drops into a little coffee cup full of water...
and then putting that in an echo chamber, several tracks of it.
And out of that comes just a subtle off screen "plink-plink" of the water.
But it makes it seem like a dank and leaky kind of subterranean place,
and it adds a coloration to the environment.
LUCAS: The advantage we have in these films...
is that it takes about 10 years to get through the trilogy.
So Mark was pretty young when we did the first one...
and he was a much more mature individual...
by the time we reached this film.
The same thing with Carrie.
With Harrison it didn't show up so much 'cause he started out, he was over 30.
But Carrie started out at 19,
and Mark was in his 30s by the time he finished.
So it allowed him to use a lot more of his maturity,
not only in the natural telling of the story,
when he becomes more mature,
but he actually was more mature as an actor and as an individual.
So I think a lot of his naive boyishness...
that existed in A New Hope just naturally...
drifted away by the time we got to this film.
He was a much more serious person.
BURTT: Another local performer from the Bay Area
who provided some important voices in this film...
for our alien characters was Erik Bauersfeld.
And he provided the voice for Bib Fortuna.
He has a refined,
almost a mild British accent,
which gave Bib an unusual sense of dignity despite his appearance.
Master.
Oohh!
FISHER: George got me up to San Francisco and showed me...
a picture of this bathing suit...
to frighten me into exercise, I think.
He succeeded.
This is the other thing, too, that they wanted-
"Do not have any lines on the side of you."
You know, there's no wrinkles on your body.
So there can be no line, like I'd been bending in half or-
So I can do that, right there.
Remain motionless, sit up really unnaturally straight...
and do not speak at all.
That's it. No "hello. ""Hey, hi! Look! I'm in a bathing suit made of iron!"
LUCAS: We hadn't done a monster per se in these.
We had the snow monster on Empire Strikes Back,
but in a lot of cases we were just moving through various genre motifs...
of fantasy and science fiction and having a little bit of fun.
BURTT: The growls and the roars...
of the rancor beast were derived from a very small animal.
It was actually a pet dachshund...
owned by a neighbour of mine.
Despite the fact that it was a cute little dog,
it had a certain vicious streak in it.
And I went over one day...
and recorded this dachshund as it growled and hissed and got angry,
and we took that sound and pitched it down much lower.
Pal was a dangerous little old guy, but he made a good rancor beast.
MUREN: This is a hand puppet...
with Phil Tippett's arm- someone's arm up in it...
and hand controlling the head up there with some mechanics inside of it.
It was probably about 15, 16 inches high...
and the arms are controlled internally,
and going straight out of the elbows to the back,
which we kept very, very dark.
The eyes, which were so important to this, are actually ball bearings.
The only way you could really ever focus on it being living...
was to look at the eyes,
and the regular eyes we made for it didn't show up,
so we just got the shiniest little eyes we could get
so you would recognise it as being a living thing.
And we took this approach, as opposed to stop-mo or something,
as a way of saving money.
And we could do this pretty quick-
we shot the whole sequence probably in-
Once we figured out how to do it, I would just have guessed
a couple weeks, which really wasn't too bad.
The rancor was in the little miniature set.
The lighting effect, the smoke effect was all done in the camera.
The only time we did any other work to the shot
was if we had to put Luke into it.
So it was really fairly straightforward to shoot.
It took a lot of takes-sometimes we'd be up to 60 or 70 takes on it.
Sometimes we shot backwards.
Sometimes we shot the scenes at different speeds-
high speed or normal speed.
Anything to get it away from that sort of familiar Muppet look
we're all used to seeing.
But the problem is, you could never get a wide view of it
because there were no legs that you could ever really see.
Except we did leg shots,
but you couldn't really see it when you saw the body...
because that's where you would see, you know,
Phil's elbow or something down there.
The drool helped make it look kinda creepy.
You know. 'Cause you're like, "Just stay away from that thing."
And I think it was, you know, sort of successful.
It suffered from what any of these sort of robotic or hand puppets
tend to suffer from, in my point of view-
is they just don't have quite the performance that you'd like to have.
'Cause you're so, so limited because you're working with such problems
to try to get the thing to even move.
And one of the great things that's come with computer graphics is...
that the performance is as good as your imagination is,
so you can make the things look much, much more interesting...
and their performance much more elaborate and much more fun to watch.
LUCAS: I like the idea that everybody loves somebody.
Even though this was the worst big, horrible monster
you could possibly imagine, his keeper loved him.
[CHUCKLES] And he probably loved his keeper.
BURTT: Another one of our favourite voice talents in the Bay Area...
is a guy named Ernie Fosselius.
He was most famous for doing the film Hardware Wars,
a spoof of Star Wars many years ago.
But Ernie would come in whenever we needed a good comic voice.
He did a great job of doing the voice of the rancor trainers. He did both of them.
I remember telling Ernie to kind of take the approach that it's Stan Laurel...
with that kind of whimpery cry.
Ernie's the kind of guy that can practically imitate anything.
Ernie created a really funny moment with that character.
LUCAS: Another aspect of these films that sometimes gets lost...
is that these films are vaguely light-hearted...
in terms of the fact they don't take themselves that seriously.
They try to be very realistic and everything,
but mostly it's to have a rip-roaring adventure...
and a good time.
Sometimes people try to put more into them than should be there...
or write them off as of no consequence,
but the reality, I think, is somewhere in the middle.
Again, with this movie we got a chance to expand the universe a bit,
see more Banthas than just one or two, see them in their natural environment.
There's so little time in these movies to show the environments...
because we're really pushing the story all the time.
It's great when you get another opportunity
to push the camera over here and say,
"Oh, look what's over there."
MUREN: This is the barge sequence-
one of the sequences Richard Edlund supervised.
It was a pretty tough sequence. Looks pretty neat.
The backgrounds, I believe, were all shot in Yuma, Arizona.
The ship were primarily models.
Sometimes some of the little transporter ones on the side
were about two feet wide...
and the main barge was about six feet long, something like that,
shot against bluescreen and combined with the background.
Then there was bluescreen photography, also,
shot on the stage here, where the actors are shot blue at ILM
It's very hard to get this- It was in those days, especially,
to get anything to look like it was really sunlight when it was shot indoors.
And to pull the mattes of fin this,

BURTT: We sent a sound crew out into the dunes...
while these scenes were being shot.
The main thing we got was sand in our equipment. [CHUCKLES]
It was so windy, so much blowing sand, that it was actually quite dangerous...
with all the gear that we took down there.
And we ended up actually not spending too much time on the set...
getting sand in our equipment,
but rather we went over to a local naval air station...
and recorded jets practicing landing and takeoff...
and got a lot of really great sounds which became the basis...

FISHER: We had, daily, stuntmen break things.
I would come on the set and there'd be...
this kind of... silence...
that you know means something bad.
Dave Tomblin, who was a fantastic guy- He was the first AD.

[IMITATING COCKNEY ACCENT] "George, we're talking about two men
might have a sprained ankle. Might have a broken leg."
Silence while they're waiting to find out from the nurse.

BURTT: I remember making the sound of Luke
bouncing off the end of the plank...
by just snapping a ruler on the edge of a desk-a wooden ruler.
Something I learned in fifth grade, actually.
You can make a great sound
by twanging a ruler on the edge of your desk.
Of course, you get in trouble with the teacher, as I did.
But it provided me with an idea later on
for a quick sound effect when I needed it.
LUCAS: In the case of Boba Fett's death,
had I known he was gonna turn into such a popular character,
I probably would have made it a little bit more exciting.
Boba Fett was just another one of the minions,
another one of the bounty hunters and bad guys.
But he became such a favourite of everybody's.
For having such a small part, he had a very large presence.
And now that his history has been told in the first trilogy,
it makes it even more of a misstep...
that we wouldn't make more out of the event of his defeat.
Because most people don't believe he died anyway.
I had contemplated putting in an extra shot where he climbs out of the hole,
but I figured it doesn't quite fit, in the end.
The main character, ultimately, that dies in this scene is Jabba the Hutt.
As much as some people hate the cute little Ewoks,
they love the relationship between Princess Leia and the slug.
FISHER: He was made of Styrofoam, I believe,
and they asked me if I wanted a stunt double to kill him.

BURTT: The battle with the Sarlacc monster...
was one of the last scenes that we mixed in the film,
because I had gone through a lot of editorial changes...
and there were different versions of it...
kind of each week as we were getting later and later
into the post-production schedule.
It was a very challenging sequence editorially...
because, typical with a Star Wars action sequence,
there were so many different characters involved,
all with their own simultaneous stories.
And to sort it out, and to keep the flow of action clear and continuous...
there was quite a bit of re-cutting of this scene.
In fact, it was scored once in London by Johnny Williams...
and then the sequence was recut so many times after that...
that they had to go back and re-score it...
with a different cue of music.
LUCAS: I'm not sure we ever really did it as well as we could have.
But when you have so many different pieces of action...
that are supposed to be happening at exactly the same moment,
you get into a very complex piece of cinema,
which is editing things together that you want people to read...
but you don't want to dwell on them for so long...
that it drags down a simultaneous moment.
I think there's always a kind of super-reality...
that's created in a moment like this where you're stretching time...
and hoping that it doesn't look like that.
This is something Steven Spielberg is very accomplished at.
The best project I ever worked on where that happened...
is in Raiders of the Lost Ark under the Flying Wing.
It's exactly like this sequence...
where you got a truck full of gas and some guys loading the truck.
Then you got a fight. And then you got a pilot.
Then you got people coming from the outside.
Then the truck catches on fire. Then it's going toward the airplane.
Then the girl gets trapped in the airplane.
It's very similar to this.
In that case I think it's done as well as it can ever be done.
But in this case we did it as well as we could, but...
I always had this nagging feeling that we could have done it better.
It's a very delicate balance of having the audience be able to follow it...
and not having it look corny.
BURTT: The explosion of Jabba's barge...
was probably one of the bigger
and more prolonged explosions that we've put in the films.
Everything was thrown into it.
Probably the kitchen sink was thrown into it.
We had several explosions, a lot of fire.
And there was sounds of rockets we had recorded.
And we used that for the sounds of the debris going by camera.
We had gone once to a house in Petaluma, California
that was being torn down.
All the furniture and fittings were in the house...
and they said we could do anything we wanted in this house.
So, I spent an entire day there. We dubbed the place "Junk Heaven"...
because we went around smashing appliances,
breaking windows, throwing things down the stairs,
taking dressers, furniture, tumbling it out second-story windows.
Opening up kitchen cabinets and smashing dishes,
throwing things around.
Since it was all done in a place, it sounded real.
It wasn't like a Foley stage where it sometimes can be acoustically artificial.
What you want is that kind of spontaneity about it so it's not perfect.
MUREN: There was more of everything. There's more spaceships.
There's more characters.
The docking bays that the ships land in are bigger.
There's a shot that was reused from Empire.
And as the ships go in,
you see there's a lot more
than you would have seen in the earlier films.
And a lot more detail inside.
Just more, more-Bigger.
So you get a sense that the battle is on a massive scale.
And the docking bay here-
Every one of these shows has a docking bay.
And they all have reflective floors on them.
This is the biggest one, being the last of the first three movies.
And pretty neat. And all these characters here-
It sort of gives you an idea what George was thinking...
when he got to Phantom Menace, what the scale of that film would be.
He was really building it up to just really huge, huge epic proportions.
See, in the background of these shots, they actually probably had...
two or three dozen actors
and they would populate them as much as they could.
Then we would fill in the rest, either with paintings
