LUCAS: The emperor's coming to visit the Death Star,
so it was like a big parade of all of their weaponry and their troops.
It's like May Day in Russia.
In terms of a display of force, this is as big as it has been at this point.
I was able to actually...
get a more cosmic view of the galaxy by bringing the emperor into this...
who, even though he's sort of the overlord of the universe,
is always in the background of the movies.
In this one, I had the opportunity to bring him into the forefront...
because it ultimately becomes a confrontation...
between Vader and the emperor,
which is in the background of Episodes IV and V...
but very much in the foreground in this film.
Because that's what the real conflict is.
The real conflict is with the real evil master who is the emperor.
And he's referred to, but we never really get to know him.
One of the advantages of being in the third act...
is that you get to tie up all of the loose ends.
But one of the problems you have in the third act...
is that you have to tie up all the loose ends.
The story is pretty much dictated by everything that's gone before it...
and it's really a matter of how well you're able to fit in all the resolutions...
and keep the story going forward.
That's usually what ties you up here...
'cause you end up with too many things to try to solve...
and not enough time to do it in.
In coming back to see Yoda, we have to figure out Luke's training...
and the fact that he never finished his training...
and that obviously now he's got a big question he wants answered.
There is a point where the hero has to be left alone on his own two feet...
without anybody there to help him.
And you can sort of have him be in a different place or something.
But at some point you have to say, now all the props have been taken away...
and he has to face the evil monster alone.
In this case, this scene establishes that the evil monster is actually his father...
and he's gonna have to do it on his own
and that he's not really equipped to do it,
that he was too impatient,
he didn't finish his studies and now he's gonna be half-trained...
to face a difficult physical and emotional challenge.
Then I am a Jedi.
Oh! [COUGHING]
Not yet.
One thing remains-
Vader.
When I did the end of the last movie, The Empire Strikes Back,
one of the issues that came up...
in terms of my concern about how kids especially...
would take Darth Vader revealing that he is Luke's father...
was most of the psychologists said that most kids would just deny it
and say it's not true.
So, I had to reaffirm in the audience's mind...
and in Luke's mind that it is true, and now he has to deal with it.
Yoda, I must know.
Going into this movie,
I had to assume that at least half of the people didn't believe it.
So I had to make it a true thing that had happened.
Traditionally, in the original long version of the script...
this is where Obi-Wan Kenobi dies and leaves Luke alone.
There's always an advantage to the third film...
in both of these trilogies, the Star Wars trilogies,
which is that it starts out with a young boy, naive youth,
and by the time we get to the third film he's an experienced warrior,
he's matured into his full self...
and therefore he's always a much more interesting character.
Because he's richer, and he's ready to take on the world...
and his final challenge.
It's one of the things, also, why the third chapter...
or the third act of a play is more interesting...
because the first one you introduce the characters,
the second, you introduce the problem
and the third you get to resolve everything.
And this being the third part,
it has all the resolutions of all the various problems...
that had been developed in the first two films.
The center of the movie is a lot of exposition.
It's a lot of explaining what has happened...
and why things are the way they are.
In this particular case, there's a whole issue of Luke's sister...
and, you know, the fact that they were twins,
which is an element that has to be revealed...
which comes in later in terms of...
who is the "other" that was talked about in Empire Strikes Back...
and how could that person become as powerful as Luke?
Well, if they're twins, then if she were trained and...
she has the same abilities Luke has,
that becomes an important issue,
especially in terms of resolving
all the love triangles that have been going on.
BURTT: I had the opportunity to go to London...
for the filming of Return of the Jedi...
to supervise any sound recording that was being done on the set,
and also to gather sound effects while I was there...
and help develop the languages for the Ewoks...
and many of the people in Jabba's palace.
I remember going on the set when they weren't filming.
They were off on some other set that day.
But Jabba the Hutt, the big puppet,
was sitting there empty on the set all by itself.
I crawled up inside with my tape recorder...
and recorded the little motors, the little servomotors...
which made his eyeballs move back and forth.
And we got a lot of mileage out of those little motors.
The sound of the thermal detonator which Boushh holds in his hand.
Those little motors-some of those are from the actual eyeball motors...
that were inside the Jabba puppet.
Return of the Jedi was the first mix...
that we did entirely in Northern California.
The previous two episodes were edited and sound designed up north,
but we took all the materials to Hollywood...
where we had a lot of talented people at Goldwyn Studios...
help us put it together and do the final mix.
But when Jedi came along we had for the first time
our own complete mixing facility...
in San Rafael, California.
Therefore we were able to exercise...
almost a complete technical and creative control over the soundtrack.
It was a new experience for all of us.
Gary Summers and I mixed this film-
and I think it really was the first feature film we ever mixed.
I think this was really the first film
which was technically watched over by Tom Holman...
and ultimately what became the THX and TAP program.
There was a great deal of engineering expertise...
that went into watching over just the technical specifications
for doing the sound mix for this film.
Normally you mix a film...
from the beginning to the end in the order that the scenes occur.
But because this film was being put together...
when we started the mix of the movie, the final mix,
we actually began work in the middle of the movie.
We started with reel five.
And then, later, we went back and did the beginning of the film.
It's not a great way to work- doing it out of context-
because there's always a natural flow and feel for the sound
as it develops throughout the movie.
But we had no choice because there were certain scenes...
that hadn't come together yet.
The special effects were not completed enough in many scenes...
to warrant mixing the first few reels of the movie.
LUCAS: This briefing room scene is what we call the "pointer scene."
A pointer scene is where you sit down and tell the audience...
all the things that have to happen, or are going to happen
in the rest of the movie so they can follow the plot.
We have to go to the castle. This is the way we get to the castle.
These are the dangers we have to overcome.
Then there's the prize at the end.
And there is in almost every movie a pointer scene...
where everybody sits down and says, well, this is what's gonna happen.
In some scenes it's just very blatant. They have a chalkboard and a pointer.
They draw a diagram and say this is what we have to do.
All these scenes are pointer scenes. You can see in A New Hope,
when we did the pointer scene toward the end about the Death Star,
that was pretty much the state of the art
in terms of computer animation at that point.
Now, in this film we pretty much have the same scene,
but computer animation has advanced quite a bit.
Both of those were done through the use of computer animation-
the original Death Star illustration and this particular new Death Star.
Part of the advancing technology is it allows you to do more things,
to have a more epic quality to things...
which gets more sophisticated as you go along,
both because you can master the technology...
and because you have a better grasp...
of literally just how to do things.
How to get more people in masks.
There's a whole science behind people acting in masks.
They can't stay in the mask very long.
It's hard for them to breathe. Some people get claustrophobic.
Again, on each one of these characters...
there's two or three people with remote controls moving the eyes.
For every one of these people there's three or four other people...
standing on the sidelines trying to operate them.
So, if you have three or four puppet characters...
you have a huge number of people on the side
trying to make them all work.
In each film, we were able to add more and more people in...
because we got better at doing that sort of thing.
The story progresses...
from being a very contained environment in A New Hope...
to a bigger environment in The Empire Strikes Back.
And then, obviously, when we get to Return of the Jedi...
we're able to have large fleets of ships,
more exotic environments, more stormtroopers.
We've learned how to build the scale up,
you know, while still staying within our financial resources.
Sort of more frequencies on the transmitters.
Pushing the edge of special effects in order to tell a more interesting story,
you're always sort of on the edge of having it not work.
And then the whole film falls apart.
Because it is a facade. It's an illusion.
Especially a fantasy film of any kind.
You're creating a fantastic illusion that people know isn't real.
So you have to make it kind of...
super-believable, even though it's impossible
and can't possibly be real.
We've used about every possible special effects technique...
you can imagine over the years in these movies.
Almost everything that is doable...
and has been done is represented someplace.
Which is one of the reasons everybody had a lot of fun doing these films...
'cause there was always a different kind of effect,
always a different challenge.
A lot of classic situations that had to be dealt with.
It was a chance to do something that doesn't come up very often.
The scene with the Millennium Falcon and Lando...
is a way of putting a little bit of an edge on the film...
that Han Solo doesn't think he's gonna ever see his ship again.
There's concern something may happen to him,
something may happen to Lando.
It brings up the possibility that some of the characters could be in jeopardy.
BURTT: We've had lots of adventures recording sound over the years.
A lot of the switches and buttons you hear inside the shuttle craft...
were actually recorded on board an old World War II submarine...
that was in dry dock, but we were allowed to go on board.
It was very quiet, but the nice thing about the submarine was...
it's a confined space with metal walls and ceiling.
So, when you turn the switches and click buttons and things...
they have a real natural, real sound to them 'cause they are real.
We had actually gone on board a sub to record a lot of material...
for Raiders of the Lost Ark.
And had a bit of a library there, and we recycled them through
the Star Wars films whenever we needed that kind of sound.
LUCAS: The emperor's outpost on the Death Star is a tall spire.
It's reminiscent of- when you see the first three trilogies-
of the Jedi temple.
In this case, the- there's one spire on the Death Star...
and the Jedi temple's got five spires.
One of the more exciting parts about making films is that...
you come up with an idea that you don't know you can accomplish...
and then, at some point, it starts to come together.
You see it on the screen the way you thought it would be, and it works.
It's a big thrill. It's especially exciting in the world of special effects...
where it's something that just can't happen...
and then suddenly you see it on the big screen, and there it is.
With performances of the actors or the characters,
there's a magic that happens when you put all the elements together...
and they're saying their lines and doing what their supposed to do...
in the right environment and it all comes to life.
It's a very rewarding feeling.
BURTT: A good sound designer always keeps his ears...
and eyes open in everyday life...
because some of the best sounds that we've recorded,
the most useful sounds, are just things you encounter sometimes by accident.
Sometimes you're on a family trip, or you're out shopping.
I've gone to stores, and I've opened the cabinet to get a soft drink
out of the refrigerator cabinet...
and I can hear a broken motor that's in the cabinet.
You-You know, I run out to the car and get the recorder and come back in
and record that funny motor.
And it becomes the sound of a speeder or something.
Those kinds of events happen all the time,
and you have to be alert to them.
Because some of the best sound effects come from things out of everyday life.
Not the kind of sounds which you very carefully orchestrate...
in a studio somewhere where it's all quiet...
and maybe it's acoustically a little bit of an artificial place,
'cause no place sounds like a studio.
Sounds in the real world are often a lot more interesting and natural...
because they come with a built-in environment connected to the sound-
a little bit of echo, the sense of a place.
Therefore a naturalness.
And part of the trick in all the sound effects in Star Wars...
is to give the film a sense of credibility.
The sound can give what is otherwise fantastic and maybe artificial...
a dimension of credibility.
And therefore using real sounds as often as possible,
recorded outside under everyday conditions,
often is the most useful kind of raw material
to orchestrate into the soundtrack.
LUCAS: The thing about Endor is that...
I wanted an environment...
that was different from the other environments.
But it needed to be a jungly kind of place.
A lot of growth and green,
which is the color of life and-
I wanted to give it a different look than Dagobah,
which in essence had the same qualities.
It was a swamp. It was, again, a cradle of life environment-lots of life there.
But I was beginning to run out of environments,
you know, something that was unique.
The only thing I could come up with was really giant sequoias...
where the trees would be so big that it would give it a different look...
than what we'd seen before.
The reason I was able to move on and do the first three films...
was because I was able to create digital environments.
By the time I got down here to Return of the Jedi,
I'd just pretty much shot everything on Earth. [CHUCKLES]
The speeder bike chase turned into a very fun experience.
I love speed and racing and chasing and that sort of stuff.
This was one of those ideas I had
when I was writing the script that I really wanted to do,
but I had no idea how we would accomplish this.
Usually, I have a guess of how I might be able to do it and-
You know, I thought maybe we could do it with miniatures or something.
We could figure out a way. I made a leap of faith and left it...
to Dennis Muren to figure it out.
Dennis came up with a very unique way...
of doing stop-motion animation in the forest environment,
which had never been done before.
And it really created these sensational backgrounds.
That was the biggest challenge here.
It was the one time in all these movies where I came up with an idea...
that I didn't know that I could pull off.
Now a scene like this with digital technology would be very easy to do.
But in those days it was next to impossible.
MUREN: This is one of the funnest sequences I worked on...
in any of the Star Wars films- the speeder bike chase.
We shot it in Eureka- north of Eureka, California...
and just slightly south of Eureka, California
where these redwood forests were.
The speeder bikes in these are pretty much most of the time models.
And then we had the actors for a week or two against bluescreen
for close-ups and medium shots.
And the models are probably about two-feet wide...
in wider shots that are motion controlled.
And the backgrounds were shot- side views were driving along in a car
on a road looking at redwoods.
The front views and back views were done with a Steadicam.
Garrett Brown, who invented the Steadicam, walking along in the woods
at about four or five miles an hour...
shooting one frame of film a second, sort of stop-motion style.
When you put them together, you end up with this feeling...
that you're going along at a hundred miles an hour
in any direction.
But it was a really neat sequence.
The sort of jumpy quality that you got with the Steadicam views,
the front views and back views, added a real energy to it.
It made it look like it was difficult to shoot, the jumping up and down on it.
It was something that George really responded to right away-
something that was unintentional, but it added a reality to it...
that you wouldn't have gotten if you'd built it as a model or something
and had a nice smooth camera going along.
It's sort of an artifact of the hundred miles an hour that you're traveling...
gave it a real dynamic to it- a documentary kind of look to it.
See, in all those backgrounds there,
you can see how they're kind of trembling, and they're never level.
All these cameras jumping around, just trying to do it.
That's just Garrett trying to do the best job he can keeping it steady.
But at one frame a second you're never gonna quite be able to do that.
BURTT: The gear shifts and the various sounds of the bikes...
changing tones as they went different speeds-
the principal sound for that came about in a funny way.
I was in England, in an office, during the production of the movie
and I was doing some paperwork.
I heard outside, coming from the street, near the studio...
a very strange sound- kind of a coughing, but musical sound.
And I went outside, and I saw some men
that were trying to repair a pothole.
And they had an air hammer that they were trying to use,
but it had gotten a rock caught in the rubber hose...
which fed the air to the hammer.
And so as they activated the hammer,
they were actually playing a funny musical instrument.
I said, "Please, don't fix it. Let me go get my recorder. I'll be right back."
Obviously they looked at me like I was pretty strange, but they tolerated it.
I came back a few moments later with my recorder.
We recorded that funny air compressor...
and used it for the sounds of the bikes shifting gears.
George Lucas came by to listen to the speeder bike chase...
when I thought it had been completed,
and he sat through it-he hadn't heard any of the sounds before.
He liked it, but he had a comment that it didn't seem quite real enough.
It didn't have quite enough presence to it.
His suggestion was that we put in the sound...
of snapping branches and leaves, and the kind of noises you'd get...
if those bikes were really zooming through the forest...
and clipping the edges of foliage and shrubs and so on.
So we very quickly put together some sounds of brush and crackling sticks...
and we made some loops of the sound- that is, continuously playing sounds-
and we went back through the speeder bike chase...
and we would ramp in those sounds so there'd be a brief burst...
of just sticks and dirt noise and grit.
LUCAS: We started with the Ewoks...
who were, in the original screenplay, Wookiees...
that I couldn't use because I'd established...
that Wookiees were a technologically advanced group of creatures.
I needed to come up with a primitive creature...
that was still using sticks and stones and spears for weapons...
and lived in a primitive society...
that would be forced to take on the highly technological Empire.
So, ultimately, I decided to keep the characters sort of fuzzy and hairy.
But instead of having long hair, I gave them short hair.
Instead of making them tall, I made them short.
So we had a lot of little people that we put in costumes.
Again, technology is limited in what we can do
to create these kind of creatures and make them real.
Mostly it depends on the personality of the actor inside the suit.
And I was fortunate to find this one Ewok,
this little boy, Warwick Davis,
who was so expressive in his suit and had such personality-
the way he walked and the way he carried himself,
the way he was able to act-
he really was a hundred percent into the character.
Again, the challenge of creating a likable character...
out of a kid in a bear suit
that actually has a personality as a character-
isn't just somebody who's there- is very difficult.
You're always pushed to how far can you go...
in having strange creatures, exotic creatures...
who are actual characters in the movie...
using this kind of, you know, "real" technology-
a real puppet or a real person in a suit.
Which was about all we had in those days. It was very limiting.
FISHER: After a while, it was very difficult for me
to do action sequences...
where I punched people and hit people.
It's not really my thing. It's stressful.
You pretend that you're shooting at people and shooting guns...
and you're upset, and you're crazed all morning long.
And so I'm revving up this thing that is not innate in me necessarily.
And I can be a pretty intense... broad.
But, you know, after a certain amount of time, I was... done.
And you can't be done. You have to keep going.
LUCAS: Warwick was so good in this that I had been thinking about doing...
another kind of more traditional fantasy that involved little people
and got him to star in that.
He's done a lot of roles since.
But that film was Willow, directed by Ron Howard.
Now, as we get into the last reels here of the film...
we're into the pulling the plot together,
which ultimately is the trap that the rebels are falling into.
The trap that Luke is falling into.
The trap that the emperor has been planning to do away
with all his enemies.
So now this takes all of the dominoes that have been set up...
in A New Hope and Empire Strikes Back
and the first part of this film...
and starts to tip them over, which in this particular case...
is reasonably complicated 'cause we have
three different stories going on-
One is in outer space with Lando
and the spaceships trying to get into the Death Star.
One is on the planet with Han Solo and Princess Leia...
trying to destroy the force field that protects the Death Star.
And then you have Luke Skywalker on the Death Star...
confronting his father and the evil head of the Galactic Empire.
I do like to tell a lot of different stories
and cut back and forth.
I'm not a big linear storyteller.
American Graffiti, there were four stories being told simultaneously.
They were all going very fast.
I like simultaneity and jumping back and forth.
That's what I'm most comfortable with, for whatever reason.
Well, my first film, THX, wasn't like that.
It was completely linear- didn't cut back and forth with anything.
But after that I changed my mind. [CHUCKLES]
BURTT: The hardest thing to create in these films for me...
is always the voices.
Sound effects is one issue. But voices are listened to...
with a very critical ear by the audience.
We're all experts at identifying voices
and understanding where they come from...
and read into all the nuances of voice quality,
intonation and performance.
The biggest voice task in Jedi was the development...
of the Ewok languages and the Ewok character voices.
The first step was to create a language.
Now, I don't create a language really by sitting down...
and trying to reason it out as to what the cultural context is...
and how I'm gonna conjugate verbs or any of that.
Generally speaking, we've worked...
by first listening to actual voice recordings...
from around the world of different languages...
until we find a language which sounds exotic, not familiar,
and has something of the character of the types of aliens
we're trying to create.
Even though we don't speak that language,
we'll listen to recordings of it...
and we'll begin to extract sounds from that language...
and eventually build up kind of a double-talk set of sounds...
in which we're mimicking this exotic language.
The Ewok language is really derivative of Kalmyk,
which is a language that comes out of Asia...
in areas of central China.
I saw a documentary on television...
that interviewed some people in this region of China-
some nomadic tribesmen-
"and I thought," Wow. This language is very interesting.
"It has something we haven't heard before.
It seems like it would be something good for the Ewoks."
We began to look around to see
if we could find anyone that spoke this language.
Of course, there wasn't offhand anybody immediately available...
since it came from a remote part of the world.
But we finally, through some research, came into contact with some refugees...
who had come from that part of China.
And in particular, an elderly woman-
she was probably in her 80s- was brought to us.
She didn't speak any English.
She had recently immigrated from primitive regions of China.
She had lived in a tribe...
as some kind of a quasi-nomadic existence all her life.
She probably didn't really understand
what we were trying to do in recording her voice for a movie.
But we approach these things...
by usually bringing the person into the studio...
and I sit down with them.
You get her to tell stories.
This is usually how I do it. A person who's not an actor...
you just don't put a microphone in front of them and say, "Okay, talk."
You usually wanna put them at ease and give them something to do.
What I found with her and what I've done with other native people...
was to sit down with them and have them tell me folk tales...
and to pretend they were telling me stories,
bedtime stories, as if I was a child.
They can tell me a story in which they may do
the voices of different characters...
and they can inject a sense of drama into what they're saying.
She wanted a bottle of vodka and, uh, we provided that for her.
And she got very lucid and gave us some wonderful sounds.
We referred to this woman as "Grandma Vodka"...
because we couldn't pronounce her real name.
And a session in the studio with her...
gave us not only the inspiration but gave us the basis...
for a lot of the Ewok voices.
We were able to take recordings that she gave us...
of her laughing, of her telling stories,
of her doing little character voices high and low...
and out of that came a range of sounds,
both male and female voices,
which provided the basis for the Ewok language.
Once we got comfortable with orchestrating those sounds...
and picking out the things we liked,
then we brought in our own staff of actors-
usually people on our own crew.
Also, people in the Bay Area that we knew...
who had interesting quality to their voices.
They either had a high-pitched voice or they had a very raspy kind of voice.
And we brought them in, played these recordings of Kalmyk...
and had them imitate it.
And we wrote out phonetically things for people to say.
We assigned particular voices we liked to certain Ewok characters...
like Wicket or the chief of the village...
or the witch doctor of the village.
And then the crowd sounds of the Ewoks were our crew members...
imitating what they had heard in these exotic languages.
And it was just all put together...
in kind of a collage that unto itself,
I guess, ended up having a consistent sound to it.
One of the favourite recordings we got from this elderly Chinese woman...
was a little song that she sang
which you'll hear several times in the Ewok village...
when they get excited or they make a decision...
as they pass the firewood.
It's probably based on some Chinese folk song. I really don't know.
LUCAS: There was a big challenge with these Ewoks...
to make them realistic and believable.
It was very hard to get much movement in the face or much individuality...
so you could recognise them as different characters...
and believe that they were threatening at all.
They're designed to be kind of cute and fuzzy little teddy bears.
I mean, they are the ones that save the day in the end.
And so they had to have this dual capacity-
one, to be kind of cute and inconsequential...
and then at the same time at the end be tough enough to conduct a war...
that you'd believe that they could actually do it.
I knew there would be a lot of, especially older kids...
that would just have a hard time buying into this idea.
But we decided to do it anyway.
Because we had so many Ewoks in this film,
we couldn't really do remote control eyes and eyelids...
because we just didn't have enough remote control mechanisms.
And we couldn't make their mouths move.
So the actors had to make the mouths move just by moving their jaw.
And actually Warwick Davis got very good at using his tongue...
and sticking his tongue out a lot and sort of moving it around.
It really gave the sense that the lips were moving
and something was going on there.
All it really was was him sticking his tongue out.
But I think there's an impression that the mouths move,
even though they actually don't.
Some of the actors got pretty good at moving their jaw.
But it was very hard to do, and it was very tiresome.
If we were doing it today,
we'd have a lot more detail in the Ewoks.
I think we'd probably do it digitally.
We'd have a couple of suits for the lead characters probably.
Most of the other ones would be done digitally.
We're coping with that now with Wookiees...
'cause there's a lot of Wookiees in the next movie.
It's the same problem we had here.
You know, where do you get enough people that tall?
In this case, it was how do you get enough people that small?
If you're gonna have a scene with several hundred...
you're sort of-it's very difficult to do.
BURTT: The scene where 3PO tells the story to the Ewoks...
of their adventures and how they got to the Ewok forest...
is one of my favourite sound effects moments.
Tony Daniels was absolutely brilliant here...
in that he did a sign language and an audio recreation...
of events in the Star Wars movies-
the walkers, the spaceships, the lightsabers.
Together, Tony and I worked out
the Ewok language that he would speak...
based on what we were developing for the Ewoks.
Tony made up a lot of the words himself
and was very clever about the whole thing.
And then we integrated into his speech the sound effects-
sound effects that were from our library of Star Wars sounds.
The idea was that 3PO was actually playing back these sounds...
over his built-in mouth speaker...
in order to articulate the story to the Ewoks.
It's a fun capsulization of the signature Star Wars sounds-
the lightsaber, the Millennium Falcon, the walkers, and so on.
We went out into the woods-
into the Redwoods here in Northern California where it was quiet-
and we did a lot of off screen yells and laughs and cries as if we were Ewoks.
We can always take our voices and pitch them up a little bit...
so that we sound smaller and cuter.
And once you get some recordings like that,
which are actually recorded out in the real woods,
the forest has a wonderful acoustic quality to it.
It's one of those places where you can really sense depth...
because of the way echoes and sounds travel in a forest.
And once again, if you take some of those real-sounding recordings...
and mix them in with those done in the studio, it has a more natural sound.
LUCAS: This again was one of those...
difficult scenes... because there's a lot of exposition.
A lot of things that need to be tied up and confronted and resolved...
which sends the film in a particular direction...
that is more about explaining things than it is about drama.
But there are times when the material will dictate what you have to do...
in order to make the film work.
So if you're into the movies and you're into what the problems are...
and who's who and what's what, then it becomes a fascinating scene.
If you're looking to follow the plot and just get on with the movie,
then it's one of those hard-to-get-through scenes.
It's the kind of thing you're always trying to balance in a movie,
which is how much exposition do you give out and-
Unfortunately, I gave out very little exposition in the first movie.
So I saved it all up pretty much till the third movie.
And at this point, you kind of had to tie all the ends together...
and have the people react to those things being tied up.
The discussion here is one of those scenes
you wanna have to never write. [LAUGHS]
It's one thing for Darth Vader to tell Luke that he's his father...
and it's another thing to have Luke tell Leia
that he has a sister and that Darth Vader is his father.
That really gets hard to swallow.
It's a real challenge to pull it off and make it come out...
with dialogue that is at least sincere and believable with honest reactions.
You got these characters that have been around for a long time.
One's telling, "By the way, you're my sister.
Darth Vader's my father, and he's also your father."
Most stories, it takes the whole movie to explain that part.
They're very carefully constructed and put together.
Here, I have to dump it out in one little scene.
The voice is strong in my family.
My father has it.
BURTT: I remember this scene of Luke and Leia...
on the Ewok balcony with great anxiety,
because I was on the set this day...
and the boom operator offered me the opportunity...
to be the boom operator on one scene, 'cause I always wanted that experience.
So I said, "Sure, I'll do it."
So this meant that I had a long fish pole, as it's called,
about eight to 10 feet long, and on the end of it's a microphone.
And we were up on the stage.
We were probably 20 feet off the ground...
on a scaffolding adjacent to this walkway...
where Luke and Leia are having their conversation.
My job was to hold this pole out from the scaffolding...
and out over their heads while they had this scene of dialogue.
Well, there was no rehearsal before they shot the scene...
so I found that I wasn't in a real comfortable position.
And when they called "Roll it," this was something like...
a two- or three-minute-long scene of the two of them talking...
in absolute quietness on the set.
And I was having trouble holding onto the mic.
It was beginning to come out of my hands and dip into the scene,
and I was so afraid I was gonna drop the fish pole...
and the mic would hit Carrie Fisher in the forehead,
therefore ruining the scene and ending my career forever in sound.
FISHER: He wears the same outfit for three flicks...
and I was complaining that...
I wear, like, six outfits...
and my mother, Natalie Portman, she wears three million.
She walks through a doorway, and there's another outfit.
It's like the Liberace of sci-fi changing of clothes.
In the first film, I wore two outfits-
one for the whole movie and one for the medal-giving.
And now there's, like, a couple of outfits.
But Harrison- just one outfit, right?
So I should not complain.
"Oh, my darling, hold me."
It was better when we were fighting.
LUCAS: In the Special Edition, there was very little done to this movie.
Most of the work was actually done in A New Hope...
because that was the one that was made under the most stress,
where we fell short of what I was really trying to get accomplished.
By the time we got down to this movie, there was very little...
that I didn't get accomplished that I wanted to get accomplished.
A lot of that has to do with the fact that this is a much more personal story.
I wasn't pushing the envelope technologically
to try to tell a story that I couldn't tell.
Because the real focus of this is really the battle...
between Luke and his father.
It's the emotional core of the picture.
BURTT: I went into Jedi knowing how much...
dialogue replacement had gone on in the previous two Star Wars films.
It was frustrating because
going through the ADR process after the fact...
is hard on everybody-it's hard on the actors, hard on the director.
It's difficult to get a natural sound to the voices...
when they're done a year later on a stage out of context.
And I wanted to try...
to make a much better effort to get a good, usable production sound.
But in most Star Wars films, there's a lot of noises on the set.
I kind of gave up getting perfect production tracks...
on the Star Wars films because...
there were always so many interfering noises.
If you had stormtroopers in the scene,
their plastic costumes made a lot of racket...
and interfered with getting intelligible dialogue.
Obviously, you have characters like Vader or Chewbacca...
in which you've got actors inside the suits trying to do the dialogue...
and the best you can get out of them is a guide track
because their voices are very muffled.
And quite often, Vader, of course-
The actor in the suit for Vader is not the voice that will be there in the final.
It's only a voice to use as a guide track on the set itself.
So for lots of reasons, the dialogue is pretty much done over...
once you get back into the editorial phase of the movie.
Probably only about 15% of the original dialogue...
recorded on the set ends up in the final movie.
LUCAS: This scene, where Luke deliberately gets himself captured...
and confronts Vader, sort of defines the rest of the movie...
in terms of the fact that it's not a chase, or one trying to escape from the other,
but it's an emotional confrontation between the two of 'em.
Luke isn't gonna run away from him. He isn't gonna fight him.
It's a whole different twist on where you might expect the movie might go.
It becomes a direct challenge that Luke has with his father to say...
"I dare you. I'm not gonna run away from this."
Which makes it very different than keeping them as villains...
or a villain-hero kind of situation.
It's "We're gonna sit down and talk about this."
Again, on a lot of levels, there's some nice twists in their relationship...
in how they confront each other, but it's not like in Empire Strikes Back...
where it was actually a physical confrontation and cutting arms off
and things like that.
This is a more emotional, talking kind of confrontation.
This film was written during the Vietnam War...
where a small group of ill-equipped people...
were able to overcome a mighty power.
It's not a new idea. Attila the Hun was able to overrun the Roman Empire.
The American colonies were able to overrun the British.
That's always the same story.
The Roman Empire had huge mechanical advantage...
and training advantage over the Huns,
but the Huns were still able to overwhelm them...
with sort of enthusiasm and their humanism
and their belief in what they were doing.
And that was the main theme for the overall downfall of the Empire,
that it was basically overcome by humanity...
in the form of cute little teddy bears.
MUREN: Here's the burst to light speed.
The original version for the first Star Wars-
I think Richard Edlund did that.
It's streak photography-
stuff he'd used a lot when he was working at Bob Abel's.
The camera moves from one position to another during one frame of film.
Then it does a slightly different camera move through the next frame of film...
but covers some of the same area it did the first time.
You end up with something looking like it's stretching and then popping off.
A lot of television commercials Abel's guys did in the late '70s and '80s...
have got that same streak photography stuff.
It became quite popular. You can sort of go back even to 2001,
which is some of the first time
that had actually been done in the slit-scan stuff...
that Trumbull brought to that show.
So here it is as the jump to hyperspeed in Star Wars. Same idea.
LUCAS: The interesting part of making movies is that...
it's very hard to understand the amount of detail...
that you have to overcome in terms of making it look easy.
You know, the amount of study and training and craft...
on everybody's part all the way down the line.
You know, and if somebody doesn't do it right-their little piece right,
then it instantly shows up on the screen and becomes a distraction.
And the amount of just plain hard work it takes to get to a particular point...
to get the lines out right, to make sure the lines are right in the first place.
In this particular scene, the Ewoks jumping on the speeders-
the problem of getting the Ewok up on the speeder-
because the Ewok is very short, and the speeder was sitting...
about three feet off the ground.
It was taller than the Ewok. How to get him up there...
just physically, and make it believable takes a lot of scratching of heads.
We actually tossed him up there. [CHUCKLES] A couple guys threw him.
MUREN: So here's another speeder bike chase going on...
using a lot of the same elements
we'd used for the major sequence earlier.
Same approaches. These are model speeders...
when you see 'em full frame that are moved along.
And mainly, the camera's doing most of the motions.
If you look at some of these backgrounds
where you're going straight ahead...
or looking straight back, you might see a little white line...
over on one side of the frame, and what that is
is a string that we had along the walkway.
We're trying to keep the camera at the correct height...
and that string represents a height of about six feet.
As the terrain changed, Garrett Brown, the Steadicam operator...
kept trying to keep the camera at that height.
So it was always, like, six feet above the ground
even though the ground was changing height.
And sometimes it shows. You see that little string going by.
But it sort of adds to the chaos of the shots.
LUCAS: There's a lot of work that goes into the design of these films...
that has to do with the actual design of the frame.
Color plays an extremely important part in all these films.
You know, red signifying evil- the red blood, the red devil.
Red as an accent color for the bad guys.
The bad guys mostly exist in a black-and-white world.
The good guys live in an organic world...
which is either browns- light browns, tans-
or greens, you know, with the blue sky and stuff.
But mostly it's the good guys are green and brown...
the bad guys are black-and-white,
which has to do as much with anything-
It really has to do with that feeling- a philosophical feeling...
of a world of absolutes.
A mechanical world where things are rigid and absolute.
They're black-and-white,
as opposed to the organic world where it's more natural.
And, you know, right, wrong, black, white.
These kind of things don't exist...
at least symbolically, in that world.
Things are more natural.
Part of this stuff, in terms of-like this where the window looks like a web,
which it does in all of the Sith and imperial stuff-
You'll see it in Darth Vader's-
In Episode IV, the TIE fighters have that same motif.
It's a motif we carry through for the bad guy.
It's designed really just to give you that feeling.
It's a symbolic motif that goes through that allows-
Say, "These are bad guys. These are the people you have to be afraid of."
And he is the spider luring the flies into his web.
'Cause he's the plotter. He's the devious one.
MUREN: The nice thing about this
is that we had so many different departments
that were all so good at what they did.
And, really, when you're looking at the Star Wars films...
you're looking at the work of a good dozen groups of people...
that are real experts at what they're doing...
and focusing on their skill set and contributing...
whether it's building the models or doing the laser blasts...
or the pyro blasts or the photography on it...
or the optical compositing that's putting all the scenes together...
or the storyboards or whatever.
They're all really a great group of people that had worked together-
By this time, we had been working together for six or eight years
or so and really had it down.
I think Jedi shows as the most polished of all the shows...
just because we really had figured out how to do it.
And all the energy on this show could go into the shot design-
coming up with designs that were different and new and fresh.
BURTT: I had the opportunity to play a very minor part in the film...
as an imperial officer in the Endor power station...
who jumps out from behind a wall at the power station...
and holds a gun on Han Solo
and then get hit with kind of what was like a toolbox...
falling into a generator room below.
My big line was "Freeze!"
Freeze!
The voice of Billy Dee Williams's co-pilot...
was recorded by a young man...
who was a foreign student from Kenya who spoke Haya,
which was one of the native languages of that country.
He came in, and we explained to him what we wanted.
I wrote out some lines of dialogue in English...
that would fit the context of the story...
and he translated them into his native tongue.
And we did a recording session with him...
and his voice was dubbed in for the co-pilot.
Of course, in most parts of the world,
nobody could understand that language.
But when this film played in Kenya...
people suddenly understood their own language in the Star Wars movie...
and got so excited about it.
Fortunately, he was saying the appropriate lines of dialogue...
that fit into the story, so it had all the more meaning...
to the people who could understand it.
MUREN: As the shows went on, we got better...
at shooting bluescreen faster so we could set the models up.
But the techniques were all pretty much the same
all the way through the shows.
What we got better at was programming.
We could program models faster, so we had these shots...
where there's hundreds of ships going by.
Like this sort of sequence here, which is a big Ken Ralston sequence.
There's one shot where he's got- I think he said...
120 separate motion-controlled spaceships in it.
It was so big what George wanted.
It came from our skill getting better and faster at being able to do the work.
There wasn't really a big breakthrough in the technology during these shows...
that I could really see.
LUCAS: In the original screenplay, this was the attack.
There was a Wookiee planet.
The end with the Death Star and the Wookiee attack...
was all together at the end of the movie.
But then when I went and did only the first movie...
I kind of took the Death Star battle out and put it into the first movie.
So that's why ultimately I had to create a second Death Star.
But that was all originally- There was only one Death Star...
and it all culminated in this one giant battle...
with the Wookiees on the planet battling the Death Star...
and this confrontation with Luke...
and Vader and the emperor.
MUREN: The walking machines that are in this sequence
are what we call the "chicken walker"...
that was in one scene of Empire Strikes Back.
It was just an idea I think that Joe Johnston just came up with.
And we just made a model and stuck it in one of the scenes...
and it got a lot of comment 'cause it was so cute-looking.
We added that background walker walking through...
just to give a sense of some action going on...
so it wasn't just sort of static- like they're patrol walkers.
It kept the sequence more interesting.
This full-size walker was out there. I wonder where that walker is today.
This location was, I believe, one that was about
to be cut down anyway for logging.
So it was all right to go in and sort of blow these trees up.
I thought it was too bad that had to happen,
but here it is on film, and at least that process entertained people...
before it actually got- just everything cut down.
BURTT: Added to the library of high-tech lasers and walkers...
we also, of course, had the weapons that the Ewoks were using-
mostly bows and arrows.
I was always greatly inspired by the movie
The Adventures of Robin Hood, the 1938 version,
which had a wonderful and very unique sound to the arrows.
They used a very particular kind of homemade arrow.
And we found someone who understood
how those arrows were made.
And when we made arrows on that pattern...
they gave a really nice zinging sound.
We recorded them and used them for the sounds of the Ewok archers.
LUCAS: This is the part of the movie
was a real struggle in terms of believability.
The style of Star Wars is a little bit light-hearted
and doesn't take itself too seriously.
But at the same time, it has to be believable and realistic...
that these funny little bears with bows and arrows...
could actually confront stormtroopers.
It's a big challenge.
You set yourself up for particular characters
and when it comes time to- the confrontation...
you hope you've given 'em enough stuff to make it work.
In this particular case, I think I established...
part of the credibility of the Ewoks by simply not having them win.
I mean, occasionally, they- Toward the end they start to win,
but they lose so much in the beginning of this battle.
What you think is gonna happen actually does happen,
which is they're absolutely no match.
It makes it more believable
that what happens later on is they do the same kind of thing.
They start to get a little bit more successful at it.
But never do they actually overcome them.
It's really the battle in the sky.
That is where the real battles go down. This is really just the battle...
to get in to blow up the protective shield generator...
which means that all the Ewoks really have to do...
is distract the troops, which is believable.
They could actually pull them away and engage them enough...
to keep them occupied while the generator is blown up.
MUREN: These were different ideas
about how you knock these machines over.
And we had some cute little things here where...
we sort of give the machines
a little sense of personality- the walking machines.
Even though they're going along and blasting things, they're not invincible.
Sometimes the simplest little things can destroy technology,
which is sort of a theme in George's movies.
Another big Ken Ralston battle scene here.
This is what Ken did for most of the show-
was doing these massive, massive battle scenes...
and so many of them way beyond anything we'd done before.
And a lot more spatial sense, where things would be way in the distance
and come up way close to the camera.
I think for some of these shots we also made even smaller models
and lengthened our track...
so the model could be a lot smaller at the beginning of a shot...
or at the end of a shot than it had been before.
LUCAS: This was a chance for us to make a more complex dogfight...
and battle in space than we did in A New Hope...
with more ships and more complicated moves.
This was taking the process of these kinds of space battles...
to, like, the furthest degree I think anybody's ever been able to take them-
I mean, using this kind of technology, which is analogue technology.
The way these are done is they're sort of run through a printer
over and over and over again.
The more ships, the more times you have to run through.
Some of the shots in this sequence took 24 hours
just to run through the printer.
So then you'd look at it, and if there was anything wrong...
one mistake made on one frame, you had to go back and redo it again.
Every time you did it, there was a chance...
that somewhere along the line you'd make a mistake...
because you were trying to make sure that all of the sequences...
were put in the printer exactly the same way
and the same frame had to line up with the same frame.
You had to do this for all the mattes and all of the shots...
and all of the objects that are in the frame.
And it got to be very, very difficult.
Looking at dailies on some of the shots in that sequence of the spaceships
got to be very traumatic...
because there was so much time and money invested.
And you just had no idea whether it was gonna turn out or not.
MUREN: So the battle is picking up. There's more explosions,
a lot of which were actually done on the location.
Trees knocked over on location. Powder charges going off on location.
We would put our walkers into 'em
and make it look like they had caused a lot the damage in the first place.
BURTT: The final battle, which involved so many elements-
It was really a collection of lots of different sound threads...
that had been running through the previous Star Wars films.
When we got to the final battle in the Ewok forest,
these elements all came together.
We had all these sets of lasers we had developed
over the course of two films prior to this-
the walker lasers, all the stormtrooper lasers and so on.
Of course, we had quite a big library of explosions now.
And we were adding to it the Ewok voices, the cries, the yells-
things we recorded out in the woods and manufactured their battle cries.
The chicken walkers were really an extract
from what had been developed...
from the four-legged walkers in The Empire Strikes Back.
It's just a higher-pitched and faster walking rhythm.
MUREN: The animation effects that are in these shows...
whether the laser's firing or the beams going out...
and meeting and shooting into a superpowered beam or something are
are really important to the Star Wars movies.
That was a whole department in itself...
that did the laser effects and the explosion effects.
It's quite a creative group, too, to be able to do that...
and have 'em be sort of interesting
and look like the beams come from way of fin the distance...
and travel right by the camera or vice versa.
LUCAS: Most of this sequence with Luke
really has to do with making him angry...
and trying to tempt him to become angry...
and Luke fighting that temptation.

There's a lot of stuff that's repeated through these movies.

One is of the father and the other is of the son.

The key issue in these movies is for a Jedi

so that when he fights his father, he's fighting in anger.
Therefore, begins to use the dark side of the Force...

That is the path to the dark side.

LUCAS: Normally when you got several stories going...
what you do is you take 'em down to the lowest point...
where everything is going wrong and everybody's in trouble.
And then you bring everybody back...
all at the same time.
They all go down at the same time, they all come back at the same time.
In this particular case, with this point
where he blows up the shield generator,
is for Luke the beginning of the lowest part...
where he just keeps going down and down and down.
And for the other characters- for Han and Lando and everybody-
it's where they start coming back up again.
So they're starting to achieve their goals,
and Luke is actually losing his goal.
Which, in terms of the storytelling, lends credibility...
to the fact that maybe Luke is gonna- maybe they'll blow up the Death Star,
maybe with the emperor in it, but Luke probably won't survive.
It adds more tension and credibility to what is going on...
when you've got one of your stories going out of sync with the other two.
One is fading while the other two are doing the normal climax
to the end of the movie.
MUREN: Now these electrical bolts that are going on here...
is a good example of our animation department.
We had some really good animators that could do lasers,
they could do electrical bolts, they could do any sort of rays like that.
And they were hand animated-
you know, drawn on paper as dark lines and then photographed...
and then comped in as the inverse of that, as the reverse...
with some nice softening effects done in the compositing
and when they're added to it.
But you can see there's a whole- There's a style to it.
There's a whole life of its own...
that the animator would bring to it.
He would try to understand the point of the shot-is it painful,
is it not painful, or whatever- and then animate accordingly.
And it's a matter of also keeping things in scale.
If these bolts are supposed to be, say, two inches wide by 15 feet long,
when you have a close-up of Luke it has to be a certain size.
When you're doing a wide shot and they're smaller in the frame,
they have to be a lot smaller in the frame
in proportion to what they really would be.
And that's something a lot of people doing this work don't understand.
They just make it sort of the same size...
and can't quite imagine what it would really be like if it was in the shot.
And our guys were certainly really good at doing that.

BURTT: As we got further along
the editorial style got much more rapid.
That is, the shots were shorter in length and more of them packed together.
The challenge for sound editing and sound design is always related to...
how many cuts there are in the movie, how rapidly it goes from shot to shot.
Because essentially, every time there's a cut in a movie...
there's perhaps a necessity to change the sound.
Either introduce a new sound or modify another one...
at the same rate that you're seeing imagery change.
So the quicker the cuts, the more effort there has to be...
in careful consideration as to how you change the sound on every cut.
Because, you know, do you have something overlap
and hang over into the next shot,
do you precede a cut with another sound,
do you have a different sound on every cut,
and if you do, will it just sound like a bunch of bumps,
brief moments of sound that are just a burst of noise?
It's so quick, you can't tell what it is.
These are all really significant issues when you are doing the sound design...
and trying to imagine what the sounds are going to be...
and it's not only a matter of coming up with the sounds...
but also sounds that will connect together.
Like music would connect.
Sound effects, it's somewhat the same idea...
that you do have to consider how things will be connected together.
